title: Kavan's 4-hit shutout keeps defending champ Texas alive at WCWS with 4-0 win over Mississippi State
description: Teagan Kavan pitched a complete-game four-hitter, Kaiah Altmeyer and Kayden Henry each belted a home run, and defending-champion Texas defeated Mississippi State 4-0 on Friday to stay alive at the Women's College World Series.  Kavan (26-6), who was the losing pitcher in the Longhorns' 6-3 loss to Tennessee on Thursday, returned to the form that led Texas to the WCWS title in 2025.
fullText:
OKLAHOMA CITY (AP) — Teagan Kavan pitched a complete-game four-hitter, Kaiah Altmeyer and Kayden Henry each belted a home run, and defending-champion Texas defeated Mississippi State 4-0 on Friday to stay alive at the Women's College World Series.

Kavan (26-6), who was the losing pitcher in the Longhorns' 6-3 loss to Tennessee on Thursday, returned to the form that led Texas to the WCWS title in 2025. On Friday, she threw only 78 pitches with two strikeouts and no walks.

Second-seeded Texas (48-12) advances to another elimination game on Sunday against the loser between Alabama and Nebraska, which face off in the winner's bracket on Saturday. Unseeded Mississippi State (43-21), playing in the WCWS for the first time, was eliminated after back-to-back shutout losses.

Altmeyer’s home run, her first of the season, gave Texas a 2-0 lead in the second inning. Henry's home run to right field was her 13th hit of the postseason, eight of them for extra bases. Henry's third-inning double to left field missed clearing the wall by just a couple of feet.

Texas also lost its opening game in the super regional against Arizona State and it took a two-run pinch-hit home run by Victoria Hunter to force a Game 3. The Longhorns then advanced with a 5-0 win in the decisive game. If the Longhorns reach the championship series, they would be trying to become the fifth team to win the title after losing their opening game.

Mississippi State starter Delainey Everett (3-2) pitched through the fourth, allowing Altmeyer's home run and just two other hits. She was the winning pitcher in both of the Bulldogs' wins in the super regional against Oklahoma, Game 1 in relief and Game 3 with a complete-game shutout. She missed much of the season with a shoulder injury and pitched only 20 innings prior to Friday.

Xiane Romero had three hits for the Bulldogs.

Mississippi State faced the two teams from the 2025 championship series — Texas and Texas Tech.

title: Three Knights sign to play collegiate soccer
description: May 29—ROYAL CITY — Positive energy filled the Royal High School commons Wednesday as Cristian Nieto, Ernesto Deras and Anderson Brown signed their letters of intent to play soccer at the college level. For Head Coach Jens Jensen, it's always great to see players excel and advance to the next level. "It's good for them. Obviously happy for them, that's what they want to do, and they have an ...
fullText:
May 29—ROYAL CITY — Positive energy filled the Royal High School commons Wednesday as Cristian Nieto, Ernesto Deras and Anderson Brown signed their letters of intent to play soccer at the college level. For Head Coach Jens Jensen, it's always great to see players excel and advance to the next level.

"It's good for them. Obviously happy for them, that's what they want to do, and they have an opportunity to do that," Jensen said.

Brown — University of Washington

In a previous interview with the Columbia Basin Herald, Brown expressed a lot of excitement when he initially announced his verbal commitment in 2025 to the University of Washington.

"It was a long decision, but little sense of relief, but also some hunger, too, to get ready to be at the division one level," he said.

Now signing day is finally here, he's thrilled to put pen to paper and make it official, he said. As he got to know the Huskies soccer program, he grew a strong feeling that it was the right fit for him.

"Right when I got on campus, the coaches were great, and the environment was great. All the players seemed to like each other, and I mean it's a great program, great coaches, great environment, great culture. Checked all my boxes," Brown said.

Jensen expressed excitement for Brown getting to join a program that won their first national championship in program history in December 2025.

"Anderson spent a lot of time and energy in club soccer trying to have this opportunity, and his parents made a lot of sacrifices," Jensen said. "He learned some really valuable lessons, and when he came to us it was really fun to plug him into an environment where he was able to be more creative."

In his final season, Brown scored 14 goals and recorded six assists

Deras and Nieto — Wenatchee Valley College

While most student-athletes say goodbye to their teammates after their senior season, Deras and Nieto will remain Knights as they continue playing together for WVCs men's soccer team.

Nieto noted that Deras encouraged him to look into the Knights program. As he learned more, the program seemed to check all the right boxes for him.

"I think it's the right option because of the culture, I like my teammates and it's not that far from home, so I could visit whenever I want," Nieto said.

For Deras, WVC was the right fit for him in similar ways to Nieto.

"I'm really excited for this new chapter," Deras said. "When I went out there to practice, I just knew it was the place. It's close to home, I could visit whenever I wanted to, and they were all nice."

In their final season at Royal, Nieto scored 27 goals and recorded four assists while Deras scored eight goals and led the team with nine assists.

As a trio, they led the team in every offensive category, Jensen said. He was emphatic that he would not take credit for reaching this point, rather it was their hard work that got them there.

"For me, if I want to take credit for anything, is that hopefully we created an environment to play the game that they love in a way that they really enjoyed," Jensen said. "That it was really fun, that they'll look back and say 'That was some of the most fun I've ever had playing soccer."

title: Congress doesn't need to clean up the mess made by college sports
description: Some in Congress remain obsessed with cleaning up the mess that college sports created through decades of their own antitrust violations.
fullText:
Some in Congress remain obsessed with cleaning up the mess that college sports created through decades of their own antitrust violations. As many who would rather Congress not get involved in their business like to say, "Doesn't Congress have better things to do?"

That's a fair reaction to the news that a bipartisan bill dubbed the "Save College Sports Act" will soon be introduced by Senator Ted Cruz (R-Texas) and Senator Maria Cantwell (D-Wash).

Let's start with the title. Save College Sports. Who says they need to be saved? And what are they being saved from, other than their own past behavior?

The current challenges trace to decades of antitrust violations flowing from a system under which the NCAA created rules that applied to otherwise independent businesses that legally couldn't come together and fix labor costs. The NCAA became the tool for the antitrust violations, allowing any/every given university to say to a player who wanted to be paid to say, "Sorry, but we can only give you free tuition, fees, room, board, books, and snacks. Them's the rules."

The new bill would, via CBS Sports, give the schools a limited antitrust exemption allowing the schools to make enforceable rules regarding eligibility and transfers. The bill also would create a hard salary cap.

While the bill would also prohibit coaches from leaving for new college jobs during a given season, it wouldn't limit coaching pay or mobility after the season concludes. Which is fundamentally unfair; why can coaches make as much as the market will bear but players can't?

The Senate's effort replaces the SCORE Act, a measure in the House of Representatives that was recently abandoned due to lack of support. The Save College Sports Act should be abandoned, too.

The colleges and universities should be expected to respect the existing antitrust laws. The best solution continues to be forming a nationwide union and engaging in collective bargaining. That would give the NCAA and its members the antitrust exemption they crave.

But the schools don't want to do that. Collective bargaining comes with a cost. To get what the schools want, they'd have to give something up in return. From money to other terms, like reduced padded practices or offseason conditioning programs.

College sports don't need to be saved from anything other than the natural consequences of decades of unchecked greed. The NCAA and its members made this mess. They should be expected to clean it up.

Because, yes, Congress absolutely has better things to do than to throw a lifeline to an industry that ignored the antitrust laws for decades and now wants a license to start doing it again.

title: As college softball's popularity swells, does it have a Caitlin Clark in its midst?
description: There are a slew of burgeoning talents in the Women's College World Series. Could any of them take the next step and ascend like the WNBA star?
fullText:
OKLAHOMA CITY — USA Softball CEO Craig Cress’ mother is like many around the country these days. The new basketball fan is often asking her son if he saw the Caitlin Clark and Indiana Fever game the other night.

It’s the type of query he’d like to hear for a star in his own line of work.

“If we could find a Caitlin Clark to come and blow the softball world up …” Cress told Yahoo Sports on Wednesday at Devon Park in between sessions of the Women’s College World Series. “We just have so many good players that are good at certain times.”

More players than ever here at USA Softball’s Devon Park are one-name entities known beyond the confines of the ballpark. The increase of interest and viewership in NCAA softball feels much like the recent explosion in women’s basketball, an already-rising entity turbo-charged by Clark’s record-setting careers at Iowa and with the WNBA’s Fever.

But softball has yet to experience the unique atomic collision of the right player at the right moment in the right scenario that puts it over the top.

Attribute-wise, Cress thinks it would take a player with the fiery, never-back-down, multi-talented assets of Lisa Fernandez. UCLA’s four-time first-team All-American “is the best player pound-for-pound that I’ve seen in our sport,” Cress said.

The spotlight wasn’t there for women’s sports in the 1990s as it is now, when a combination of name, image, likeness and social media has lifted women’s athletes to new heights. Fernandez won two national championships (1990, ’92) and three Olympic gold medals pitching for Team USA in relative quiet.

“I don’t know that there’s that one player out there,” Cress said. “I just think there’s a lot of them players out there right now.”

And while many active players share similarities with Clark, none possess all of her intangibles.

A segment of Clark’s magic stems from her logo 3-pointers, an offensive weapon few others were as audacious to attempt regularly. It was something fresh, like when Brittney Griner dunked regularly at Baylor en route to the collegiate record after no one else had.

Megan Grant claims that record-setting offensive side. The senior UCLA slugger is the most dangerous of a potent lineup designed by Fernandez, now the Bruins associate coach. Grant hit her 41st home run with a blast to right in a 6-3 loss to Alabama on Thursday. The previous record was 37, set by Arizona’s Laura Espinoza in 1995.

Clark’s feat was unique in that she tore through the NCAA record books, from the NCAA women’s record to the AIAW one and all the way to Pete Maravich’s men’s mark most believed would never fall.

She stood, and still stands, alone.

Grant can’t quite lay claim to that. The basketball reserve who won a championship with UCLA in April wasn’t the only one to do it this year. She spent much of the season chasing Oklahoma freshman Kendall Wells. And the UCLA lineup averages a gaudy and unprecedented 3.3 home runs per game.

NiJaree Canady is most closely aligned in name recognition as the sport’s first $1 million pitcher, far out-earning salaries in the professional ranks. She signed a second $1 million deal with the Texas Tech NIL collective to stay for her senior season and intends to join AUSL, the professional league backed by MLB. It’s unlikely she can carry a deep fandom to the Texas Volts the way Clark did to the Fever.

If there is a singular player that most embodies the spirit of Clark, she resides down the road from the superstar’s old stomping grounds. Jordy Frahm, who changed her name after marrying in the offseason, is the heartwarming, home-state hero story leading a program back to prominence.

“I see some similarities there because of her personality,” Cress said. “I mean, she’s very laid-back.”

Clark, a native of Des Moines, Iowa, initially told Notre Dame she would play there before reconsidering for Iowa. She led the program to its first Final Four in history and played in back-to-back championship games. She has always been unapologetically proud of her Midwest upbringing and is a lay-low homebody at heart.

Frahm, a native of Papillion, Nebraska, began her career at Oklahoma, winning two national championships with the powerhouse. Two days after winning as a sopho

Like Clark, she’s energized a state that can call her their own, growing a following around her. Though this comparison, too, can rankle feathers.

“I don’t like Iowa, so, no,” Todd Petersen, Frahm’s high school softball coach, told Yahoo Sports in pure commitment to the rivalry.

In all seriousness, though, he sees the claim as valid.

“She’s a complete movie star, whatever you want to call it, in Nebraska,” Petersen said. “I go to the home games, or we have an event — I have my own high school camp — and if I say Jordy [Frahm’s] going to be there, I’ve got these kids showing up that I don’t even think they registered for camp. It’s crazy.”

The two-time NFCA Player of the Year at Nebraska is their top ace, one of the best hitters and lifted the Cornhuskers back to the WCWS for the first time since 2013. They have never won a national title in softball, and last appeared in the championship series in 1985.

“It’s almost like a fairytale,” Petersen told Yahoo Sports. “It’s turned out great for her, and it’s interesting how that works out. But you just do the things that you want to do and what your heart says. I mean, what else can you do?”

Frahm is playing her final games this weekend, and has not signed with AUSL. She is on the national team’s 36-player list, and could return to Oklahoma City for the 2028 Olympics should she make the 15-player final roster. Her 10-inning masterclass kept Nebraska on the right side of the bracket in a 5-3 extra-innings classic on Thursday night.

That’s not to say softball is flailing without its own version of Clark. The WCWS super-regional round averaged a record 695,000 viewers, up 48% from last year and boosted by the three most watched super regional games on record. All averaged at least 1.1 million.

It’s on the heels of a record 1.3 million average viewership during the 2025 WCWS that beat out the 1.2 million the men’s College World Series averaged. The Game 1 championship series win by Texas nearly outdrew the Stanley Cup Finals.

There’s an apt comparison — the 2024 women’s championship game that featured Clark’s Iowa team drew a record 18.7 million, besting the 14.8 million who watched the men’s title game the following night. It has yet to reach that mark again.

Parity and a surprising field of eight here in Oklahoma City helps, even if people spent the week bemoaning the lack of powerhouse Oklahoma and the potential negative impact. The Sooners missed the field for the first time since 2015, a streak akin to UConn in women’s hoops, but the concern has already proven overblown.

“I think we might have answered that question with the first two games,” Cress said, referencing the 11,923 fans who attended the weekday 11 a.m. CT morning session. The night session set an attendance record with 12,605. Sessions later in the weekend are sold out.

Even so, what softball could use is a perfect storm “Clark Effect” to call its own.

title: Flagler College peaked at the right time for NCAA Division II tennis title
description: The Flagler College men's tennis team won an NCAA Division II national championship, 41 years after a second NAIA title. What was the winning formula?
fullText:
If the Flagler College men's tennis team thought the 15 days between their loss to Columbus State (Ga.) in the Peach Belt Conference championship match on April 26 and the first round of the NCAA Southeast Region II tournament was going to be a mini-vacation, coach Jonas Piibor had other ideas.

"We were finished with school, and we thought the practices were going to be a little chill," said junior Aly El Rafie. "We didn't want to go into the NCAA with our legs tired."

Instead, after playing eight matches in 26 days, the Saints were put through some rigorous paces, both on the tennis court and conditioning. The physical work came along with some intense team and individual meetings with Piibor as the team sought to overcome the sting of a third consecutive loss to Columbus, two of them in the post-season and one, in 2025, that eliminated the Saints from the NCAA tournament.

Piibor believed the potential was still there for some late-season magic.

"We addressed what we needed to improve," he said. "But time was running out."

El Rafie said the team quickly realized that Piibor wasn't kidding.

"We practiced a lot longer, and tougher than we had all season," he said. "Maybe when he started back, we didn't understand. But now we do. It was worth it."

The school's first NCAA Division II national championship was the reward, beating No. 2-ranked Barry University of Miami 4-3 in the final match in Surprise, Ariz., on May 24, overcoming a 3-2 deficit with two three-set singles match victories by juniors El Rafie and Simon Malis.

Flagler rebounded from their fourth loss of the season in the PBC finals in Columbus, Ga., and swept through the NCAA South Region back in Columbus and then the national finals to win the school's third national championship in men's tennis and their first since the second of two NAIA titles in 1985 under Peter Scott.

Flagler blanked Lander 4-0 and got its payback against Columbus 4-1 in the region to earn their ticket to Surprise.

The Saints then shut out both Queens (N.Y.) and Lubbock Christian 4-0 and eliminated top-ranked West Florida 4-2 in the semifinals to earn their spot opposite Barry in the championship, where they beat the Buccaneers for the first time in 32 years.

Flagler outscored its six NCAA opponents 24-6 and finished with a 24-4 record, the most victories since winning 28 in 1988. The Saints ended the season first in the final Intercollegiate Tennis Association Division II rankings.

The Saints did it with only one senior on the roster, captain Ilian Borlee of Belgium, and two juniors, El Rafie and Malis of the Czech Republic.

The rest of the team are freshmen and sophomores. Two freshmen, Aniss Rafiq of France and Oliver Hradilek of Slovakia, combined to go 5-0 in doubles during the national championship run.

But the three most experienced players, Borlee, El Rafie and Malis, played on the 2024 team that reached the NCAA Championship match. Borlee and Malis were 5-1 in doubles during the NCAA Tournament and El Rafie was 6-0 in singles.

Flagler also was a diverse group put together by Piibor, a three-time All-Missouri Valley Conference player for Indiana State who came to St. Augustine in 2021 after serving as the head coach at his alma mater and then at Gonzaga.

The Saints are an All-International team with nine players from Europe and El Rafie from Egypt. But backgrounds blurred for the common good.

"We played our best tennis at the end of the year," Piibor said. "The attitudes were better. They bought into what we were doing, and the overall commitment was on the same page. That wasn't always the case, and I emphasized to them that we were going to do the right things, on and off the court. They finally did that, and we started to play well from top to bottom."

The two most important victories saw the Saints get over the hump against Columbus in the region finals and Barry in the national semifinals.

The Saints had lost eight of 13 matches to Columbus since 2021, three in conference tournament finals and once in the semifinals.

Flagler then slayed another demon in beating seven-time national champion Barry in the thrilling championship match. The Saints had lost 23 matches in a row to the Bucs, dating back to the 1995 season.

Piibor wasn't concerned about his team having long memories against Barry, since there were none.

Flagler had not played the Buccaneers in a dual match since the 2022 national championship finals in Altamonte Springs (won by Barry 4-0) and the current team's only experience facing Barry was in a fall individual tournament.

"Nobody on the team had played against Barry, but we had some pretty good results in the fall tournament against some of their players,” he said. “I don't think our guys were worried about past history. We're focused more on what we do."

Columbus was another issue. The Saints swept the Cougars in three 2024 matches, the third time in the Peach Belt championship, but entered the region having lost three of four.

"We had success against them two years ago but losing to them twice this year, and in the conference, really hurt," El Rafie said. "I think that third time, we all wanted it more. After winning that match [in the region], we started to believe in ourselves again."

The Saints had little trouble getting to the championship match, outscoring three opponents 12-1.

Flagler got a huge boost when it won the doubles point against the Bucs (the team winning two of three one-set doubles matches earns a team point), as Malis and Borlee beat Riccardo Trione and Simone Cavalleri 6-3 and Rafiq and Hradilek beat Yan Kodjoed and Antonin Chapuis 6-4.

It was the first time in the NCAAs that Barry had failed to win the doubles point.

But the Bucs rallied to win three of the next four singles matches to take the lead before Malis beat Trione 6-7 (2-7), 6-1, 6-3 and El Rafie closed it out over Thomas Machado 6-2, 1-6, 7-6 (7-3).

In temperatures that topped out at 97 degrees that day, under a cloudless Arizona sky, nothing came easily for El Rafie in the final set and the tiebreaker.

El Rafie had a 4-0 lead in the third set against Machado but started cramping. Malis won his match to tie the team score at 3-3 after Machado closed the gap against El Rafie to 4-2, and he then tied it at 4-4.

For the next four games and the third-set tiebreaker, El Rafie was on his own, the weight of the Saints' national championship hopes on his shoulders.

When he started cramping, El Rafie flashed back to 2024 in Altamonte Springs when he lost to Christian Wedel of Valdosta State 6-3, 4-6, 6-3 in the deciding match after coming down with cramps.

And against Machado, El Rafie was facing an experienced, determined opponent who fought off a match point in the 11th game of the set.

"I had him [Machado] 4-0, and he came back," El Rafie said. "I had match point at 6-5 and he came back. He's a senior. He's been in a lot of tough matches in his life. It reminded me of the match I had against Valdosta State and I told myself, 'you cannot lose this again.'"

With his teammates passing him sports drinks at every opportunity, El Rafie stayed upright in the blazing Arizona heat. But when the third set went to the tiebreaker, his spirits rose a bit.

"All I needed was seven points," he said. "At that point, the heat, the cramps ... it didn't matter. I could get seven points."

El Rafie quickly went up 4-2. When the two changed courts and it was Machado's serve, El Rafie was almost glad.

"He was on a side where the sun, at that time of day, was getting in your eyes on the service toss," he said. "It was right on top of you."

Whether that was a factor or not, Machado double-faulted to give El Rafie a 5-2 lead. El Rafie won two of the last three points, breaking Machado on match point with a crosscourt forehand that Machado returned wide.

"I expected him to come back again," El Rafie said. "At first, I was shocked when he hit it wide. Then I was relieved. So many emotions hit me."

He didn't completely realize it until his teammates rushed the court and mobbed him.

Watching with satisfaction was Piibor, who had talked with his players often about pressure.

"You need to have a plan to handle those situations," he said. "We talk about being a privilege to have pressure, visualizing yourself in those moments ... 'what am I going to do at this point?' It's moments like Aly faced where the training, both mental and physical, pays dividends."

In addition to the three men's tennis national championships, Flagler has five women's NAIA national titles, under Walter Shinn from 1987 to 1991. Shinn went on to coach the men's team for 22 years.

Piibor said he had never met Peter Scott before his passing in 2021 but was well-versed in his legacy. He said Shinn has been a big supporter and was among those present when the team was welcomed home on May 26 during a celebration at the Flagler Tennis Center

"It was an emotional moment to see Walter," Piibor said. "He's a great, great man."

The Flagler men's team also will always have the distinction of being the school's first NCAA Division II national champions.

"I know the history that Flagler has in tennis and I've been connected with so many people who either played back then or followed the teams," Piibor said. "It feels good to be able to share this with them. Former players, administrators, people in the community ... it's been very humbling to have their support. Tennis is a big deal at Flagler. It feels very special to have joined those coaches to keep his history and tradition alive."

Flagler athletic director Jud Damon said winning another national championship 25 years after the women's team won its last NAIA title, is important to a Division II school with an enrollment of just over 2,400 students — even in a non-revenue-producing sport.

"Another tennis championship has been a long time coming, and we're extremely proud of the program and all it has accomplished," Damon said. "In the early days of this school, under Peter Scott and Walter Shinn, tennis put us on the map and brought us recognition. We have a history and tradition in tennis and it's important for the reputation of the school and our brand. It benefits the entire institution."

This article originally appeared on Florida Times-Union: Flagler men's tennis team returns to national prominence with DII title

title: Brooke Wells' go-ahead 3-run homer rallies Alabama past UCLA 6-3 at WCWS
description: Brooke Wells hit a go-ahead three-run homer in the sixth inning and top-seeded Alabama rallied to beat UCLA 6-3 on Thursday night in the opening round of the Women's College World Series.  Alabama (55-7) will play Saturday against the winner of the nightcap between No. 4 seed Nebraska and fifth-seeded Arkansas.  No. 8 seed UCLA (52-9) will play an elimination game against the loser on Friday.
fullText:
OKLAHOMA CITY (AP) — Brooke Wells hit a go-ahead three-run homer in the sixth inning and top-seeded Alabama rallied to beat UCLA 6-3 on Thursday night in the opening round of the Women's College World Series.

Alabama (55-7) will play Saturday against the winner of the nightcap between No. 4 seed Nebraska and fifth-seeded Arkansas. No. 8 seed UCLA (52-9) will play an elimination game against the loser on Friday.

Kristen White and Jena Young had one-out singles off UCLA starter Taylor Tinsley before Wells homered for the 24th time to break a 3-3 tie.

Young and Wells, who broke out of a 3-for-18 postseason slump, had singles leading off the first and Marlie Giles’ two-out base hit gave Alabama a 1-0 lead.

Megan Grant clubbed her NCAA-record 41st home run this season following a two-run shot by Rylee Slimp off Jocelyn Briski and UCLA grabbed a 3-1 lead in the third.

Grant tied Stacey Nuveman’s school record with the 90th of her career. She extended UCLA’s NCAA record to 202 homers on the season. It was the Bruins 47th multi-homer inning.

Young, who went 3 for 3, also drew a leadoff walk in the fourth and scored on Alexis Pupillo’s 20th homer to tie it 3-3. Pupillo and Wells gave the Crimson Tide two 20-plus home run hitters for the first time in school history.

Briski (24-3) entered with a 17-inning scoreless run in the postseason. She retired the first seven UCLA hitters, which hadn't happened in any game this season. Briski allowed three runs on six hits with nine strikeouts.

Tinsley (32-7) surrendered six runs on 10 hits and became the first Bruin to make 16 straight starts on the mound since Rachel Garcia in 2018.

UCLA lost for first time in 42 games this season when leading after four innings. The Bruins averaged 10.9 runs per game, nearly 1 1/2 more than any of the other seven remaining teams.

title: Record-tying Megan Grant homer can't save UCLA from loss to Alabama in WCWS opener
description: Megan Grant hits her record-tying 41st home run of the season, but UCLA can't stop Alabama from rallying late in a 6-3 loss at the Women's College World Series.
fullText:
Megan Grant hit her 41st home run of the season to tie a UCLA career record Thursday night, but top-seeded Alabama rallied behind a five-run surge to beat the eighth-seeded Bruins 6-3 on opening day at the Women’s College World Series.

UCLA starter Taylor Tinsley took a 3-1 lead into the fifth inning but walked leadoff batter Jena Young then surrendered a 249-foot home run to Alexis Pupillo two batters later to tie the score.

In the sixth, Alabama’s Kristen White beat out a grounder to third and Young dropped a bloop single to left, setting the stage for Brooke Wells, who drove a pitch from Tinsley 239 feet to center field to give the Crimson Tide the lead.

Grant was waiting in the on-deck circle in the seventh when pitcher Jocelyn Briski got Rylee Slimp to ground out to third to end the game.

The Bruins (52-9) will have to beat the winner of the game between Arkansas and Nebraska on Friday night to avoid elimination.

Alabama (55-7) will play the winner on Friday.

This story originally appeared in Los Angeles Times.

title: Alabama Blasts Past UCLA in Round 1 of the College World Series
description: After falling behind by two runs, Crimson Tide homers and solid pitching advances Bama to the winners bracket.
fullText:
Jocelyn Briski started off strong by striking out two and coercing a pop out on a third batter (though it sure looked like she had strike three on her as well).

Jena Young and Brooke Wells led off the bottom of the 1st with singles. After two outs, Marlie Giles came through with an RBI single for an early lead.

.@AlabamaSB is fired up for the early lead 😤#WCWS x 🎥 ESPN pic.twitter.com/6hWMRm7Q7c

— NCAA Softball (@NCAASoftball) May 28, 2026

Two more Bruins whiffed in the 2nd. With one out in the 3rd, a Bruin broke up the no-hitter with a liner down into the left field corner. The next batter hit a sky-high pop-up that caused catcher Giles and first baseman Wells to collide, giving the Tide first baseman a bloody lip. After a delay to attend to her, Briski was suddenly struggling with her control. The Tide ace would give up back-to-back homers to UCLA to make it 3-1 Bruins. But then just like that, Briski got it back and continued to mow down the competition.

Bama left Young stranded on third base in the 3rd frame and put two on base in the 4th but could do nothing with it. However, they were hitting the ball pretty hard.

Young reached base for the third straight time in the 5th on a walk. Who else but Alexis Pupillo got Bama right back in the game? She smacked an offering over the right field wall to tie it up.

Pupillo with the POWER 💪 TIE BALL GAME #WCWS x 🎥 ESPN / @AlabamaSB pic.twitter.com/PtZCu0wOF6

— NCAA Softball (@NCAASoftball) May 29, 2026

With two outs in that same inning, Giles singled and Audrey Vandagriff doubled. Ambrey Taylor scorched a ball but unfortunately right at the left fielder.

In the 6th, Kristen White beat out an infield slap single and Young collected her third hit of the game. Wells walked to the plate and stroked a three run homer to put the Tide up by three.

BROOKE WELLS WITH THE 3-RUN BOMB 💣#WCWS x 🎥 ESPN / @AlabamaSB pic.twitter.com/9V6DJRgGrF

— NCAA Softball (@NCAASoftball) May 29, 2026

Briski (24-3) had that one bad span but bounced back in a big way with nine strikeouts against the nation’s most potent offense.

Early in the game on at least two plate appearances, Alabama hitters were clearly instructed to take a pitch on 3-0, only to watch the offering go right down the middle. This Bama team is not like others. It would be preferable if Patrick Murphy trusted his hitters and let them swing away. For the record, the Crimson Tide hitters amassed only two walks.

Alabama had two occasions where fielders collided trying to catch a fly ball. This deep into the season, communications should not be an issue. This is first day stuff. Someone needs to take charge and fielders need to yell out their “I got it”! The announcers tried to excuse it away due to noise in the stadium, but this game was no louder than games at Rhoads or on the road against most SEC teams.

While the noise level from the UCLA dugout seemed high, the Bama bench seemed awful quiet for awhile. Pupillo’s homer sent a jolt through the team and woke everyone up.

Similar to the Super Regional, Alabama had more success at the plate when they swung early in the count.

Briski hit a batter in the 7th, but we never saw a good replay. It sure didn’t look like the batter made an attempt to get out of the way.

Abby Duchscherer, Larissa Preuitt, and Lauren Johnson were late game defensive replacements.

As a team, the Tide has 99 home runs this season, putting the team five shy of tying the single season record of 104 set in 2010.

If Wikipedia is not lying and you include his one season as head coach at Northwest Missouri State, this win puts Patrick Murphy at 1,400 career victories.

WELLS: 2-4, 1 RUN, 3 RBI, hit the ball hard in one out as well.

PUP: 1-4, 1 RUN, HR, 2 RBI and for waking up the team.

YOUNG: 3-3, 3 RUNS, 1 BB, on-base all four plate appearances, a perfect game from a leadoff hitter.

BRISKI: 7.0 Inn, 6 hits and 3 runs allowed, 9 K, 0 BB, WIN

title: Where Matt Campbell lands on CBS Sports’ list of top Power 4 coaches
description: CBS Sports released a list of the top-25 Power Four coaches in college football, with Penn State HC Matt Campbell finishing behind James Franklin.
fullText:
Penn State football has produced a long line of successful coaches over the past 60 years. James Franklin was given the honor of leading the program before his firing last season, and now Matt Campbell is getting that same opportunity. Although the two men likely don’t have much of a relationship, their names will be forever tethered because of the controversy surrounding the coaching change.

Campbell had unheralded success at Iowa State before coming over to Penn State, leading the Cyclones to as many winning seasons in 10 years as they had in the 30 years combined before he took over as head coach. Franklin’s time with the Nittany Lions was ultimately connected to his inability to get them close to a national championship, but he flaunted regular season dominance that the team hadn’t experienced in decades.

They are great coaches who both unsurprisingly found themselves on CBS Sports’ list of the top-25 Power Four coaches. There is sure to be some clamoring over Campbell’s placement at No. 16, which is three spots behind Franklin at No. 13.

The rankings are somewhat understandable considering that Penn State’s new coach has never worked for such a big school with this many expectations. He overachieved at Iowa State, but it will be a whole new ball game with the Nittany Lions, as Campbell will have to face much better competition and more pressure.

While you can certainly make the argument that he’s a better coach than Franklin, CBS Sports gives the nod to the latter based on his experience and career success. Campbell has already achieved

title: SEC presidents, chancellors release statement opposing pooling media rights
description: The SEC opposes part of a new proposed bipartisan bill that would allow conferences to pool media rights for college sports broadcasts.
fullText:
MIRAMAR BEACH − As the SEC concluded its spring meetings on May 28 at the Sandestin Resort, presidents and chancellors released a joint statement opposing the pooling of media rights.

The Cruz-Cantwell bill −a bipartisan bill presented by Sens. Ted Cruz (R, Texas) and Maria Cantwell (D, Washington) − includes a provision that would rework the Sports Broadcasting Act to allow conferences to pool their TV rights. Specifically, conferences can pool rights up to 75% of the FBS membership, which will increase to 138 teams in 2026 with the addition of Sacramento State and North Dakota State.

The SEC entered a landmark 10-year, $3 billion media rights agreement with Disney and ESPN which started in 2024-25. The deal makes ABC and ESPN the exclusive broadcast homes for SEC football and men's basketball.

"The Southeastern Conference recognizes and appreciates the many ongoing discussions regarding potential, system-wide improvements to ensure the future success of college athletics," the statement read.

"The SEC has been intentional, through years of thoughtful planning and decision-making, in strategically positioning itself for future media negotiations. The Conference must retain the ability to act in the best interests of its membership. As such, the SEC does not support assigning the media rights to a third party and remains firmly committed to independently conducting its own media negotiations."

SEC commissioner Greg Sankey said he finished reading the 111-page bill on the night of May 27, which includes limiting student-athletes to one unrestricted transfer over five years while prohibiting midseason coaching movement.

"We have folks working and analyzing things," Sankey said. "The top line questions will be: Does the bill provide us with the necessary protections? ...Figure out what it means for college athletics broadly and what it means for the Southeastern Conference in particular."

Kevin Brockway is The Gainesville Sun’s Florida beat writer. Contact him at kbrockway@gannett.com. Follow him on X @KevinBrockwayG1.

This article originally appeared on The Gainesville Sun: SEC opposes part of new bill that would pool media rights

title: Is Auburn bound for Omaha? Experts weigh in before regionals begin
description: Auburn baseball begins their quest for their seventh College World Series this week, and they have many qualities to get them there.
fullText:
Auburn baseball performed well this season, winning 38 games against the nation's toughest schedule and winning 26 of its 39 games against Quad 1 and Quad 2 competition. Most of their success can be attributed to their pitching staff, which features two 100-plus strikeout throwers and a quality bullpen known for limiting runs late in games.

Auburn has many strong qualities that will get them to Omaha, but what is the likelihood that they will actually reach their ultimate goal? Experts at D1Baseball are high on the Tigers, with six of nine writers on the D1Baseball Regionals Roundtable picking Auburn to appear in their seventh College World Series, and its third since 2019.

The experts at D1Baseball say the best pitching rotation, best bullpen, and best defense all belong to Auburn in the upcoming Auburn Regional, three aspects that the Tigers will definitely use to their advantage. However, several members of the D1Baseball Regionals Roundtable believe Auburn has an easy journey to the top, especially in the regional round.

"The Tigers got a No. 2 seed that we projected as a 3 (UCF), a No. 3 seed that has dealt with major pitching injuries and should have been one of the last four teams in the field (NC State), and a No. 4 seed with a losing overall record and an RPI in the 230s (Milwaukee). This feels like a considerably friendlier draw than the other top-eight seeds received, particularly No. 5 North Carolina, whose 4-seed (VCU) has a No. 84 RPI, and whose 2 and 3 seeds are also formidable," writes Aaron Fitt, who listed Auburn's draw as the softest of the regional round.

Not everything surrounding Auburn is doom and gloom, however. Kendall Rogers not only has Auburn in the College World Series, but projects the Tigers to win the whole thing. He says he hopped on Auburn's hype train during the Tigers' weekend series at Texas A&M earlier this month.

"I wrote about this in College Station a few weeks ago, but goodness, I just love the vibe with this team right now. The Tigers have a potent offensive lineup that is hitting for more power, and it’s hard to find many rotations you believe in more than the Tigers," Rogers writes.

Auburn baseball's journey to Omaha and the College World Series begins Friday at noon CT against the Milwaukee Panthers, winners of the Horizon League Tournament. Check out the full schedule for this weekend's Auburn Regional here.

Contact/Follow us @TheAuburnWire on  X (Twitter), and like our page on Facebook to follow ongoing coverage of Auburn news, notes, and opinions. You can also follow Taylor on Twitter @TaylorJones__
